exemplar.title: Creating a cookbook with hand-drawn visuals and a hand-drawn cover.

detailedExemplarData.title: Creating a cookbook with hand-drawn visuals and a hand-drawn cover.

strengths: You correctly identify creative thinking and communication as key ATL skills for your learning goal and link them logically to your objectives.
Detailed examples and visual evidence (e.g., brainstorming workspace photograph) effectively demonstrate how creative thinking influenced your process.
Organisation and media literacy are appropriately highlighted for your product goal, with clear descriptions of systematic planning and design analysis.
The visual calendar substantiates your organisational strategies, showing concrete management of timelines and tasks.
weaknesses: The creative thinking section is comprehensive but overly verbose; focus on two to three pivotal instances where this skill had the greatest impact.
Your media literacy reflection describes cookbook analysis at a high level; strengthen it by specifying evaluation criteria (e.g., layout hierarchy, tone consistency, illustration style).
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: Your application of ATL skills is well-evidenced through examples and visuals, showing thoughtful integration into both learning and product goals. Refining the narrative for conciseness and specifying analytical criteria will make your reflections more focused and impactful.

strengths: Your diagram effectively connects personal interests to both learning and product goals, offering clear visual justification for the projectâs direction.
You articulate dual learning and product goals with clarity, distinguishing between mastering hand-drawn elements and sourcing recipes, demonstrating strong understanding of scope.
The product goal description is concise and focused, specifying a multicultural digital cookbook with hand-drawn elements and a clear target audience.
The extended action plan breaks tasks into detailed, sequenced steps with dates and completion status, reflecting thorough resource and time management.
weaknesses: Success criteria for recipe usefulness are split across two tables and lack cohesion; they need to be consolidated into one comprehensive, coherent table.
Learning goal criteria are not sufficiently measurableâconsider defining target improvements (e.g., number of refined sketches or specific drawing techniques).
The âuseful recipesâ row is incomplete; all achievement levels should be fully defined to ensure each criterion is testable.
The initial action plan is underdeveloped, showing only one task; ensure all planned tasks, dates, and statuses are presented in a single, ordered table.
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: The planning section demonstrates strong conceptualization and sequencing: goals are clear, visually supported, and the extended action plan is well-structured. To strengthen this further, success criteria should be consolidated and made fully measurable, and all planning elements must be cohesively presented from the outset.

strengths: You demonstrate strong self-awareness by honestly discussing procrastination and its effects, an important step for setting realistic future timelines.
Reflection on finding inspirationâthrough mentorship and developing a unique drawing styleâhighlights sustained motivation.
The usability evaluation, supported by diverse interview feedback, is effectively linked to your success criteria.
You thoughtfully articulate personal growth in creative thinking, communication, organisation, and media literacy, showing clear evidence of skill development.
Evidence of knowledge expansion (book design, illustration techniques, cultural research) is well supported by sample pages and table of contents screenshots.
weaknesses: While you note overcoming procrastination with a new schedule, you do not explicitly link this revised approach to measurable improvements in workflow or recovery time.
Your evaluation of recipe usefulness reports mixed feedback but lacks actionable recommendationsâidentify specific enhancements to address user concerns.
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: Your reflections display mature self-analysis and demonstrate clear learning gains, supported by concrete evidence. To deepen this section, explicitly connect corrective strategies to observed improvements and propose targeted actions for refining recipe usefulness.

annotations.comment: Your honest discussion of procrastination and its impact indicates strong self-awareness, which is vital for setting realistic timelines in future projects.
Your reflection on finding inspiration, especially through mentorship and developing a unique drawing style, highlights how motivation sustained your engagement.
Your diagram effectively links personal interests to both learning and product goals, providing strong visual justification for your projectâs direction.
You clearly articulate a dual learning and product goal, distinguishing between mastering hand-drawn elements and sourcing recipes. This clarity demonstrates a strong understanding of project scope.
Success criteria for recipe usefulness appear in two separate tables and lack cohesion. Consolidate and clarify these criteria in one comprehensive table.
Consider refining your learning goal with measurable criteria, such as target improvement in drawing techniques or number of refined sketches, to make progress tracking more precise.
The âuseful recipesâ row in your success criteria table is incomplete. Define all achievement levels clearly to ensure fully measurable and testable criteria.
Your product goal description is concise, specifying a multicultural digital cookbook with hand-drawn elements and clear target audience, demonstrating focused planning.
The Action Plan initially shows only one task. Ensure all planned tasks with dates and completion status are presented in a single, ordered table.
The extended Action Plan on page 5 breaks tasks into clear, detailed steps with dates and completion status, demonstrating thorough sequencing and resource planning.
You correctly identify creative thinking and communication as key ATL skills for achieving your learning goal, establishing a strong link to your objective.
You provide detailed examples of applying creative thinking during brainstorming and sketch iterations, effectively demonstrating its impact on your learning process.
The inclusion of a photograph of your brainstorming workspace (Figure 2) offers authentic evidence of your creative thinking process in action.
The creative thinking section is comprehensive but verbose. Condense by highlighting two to three specific instances where this skill directly enhanced your outcomes.
You clearly identify organisation and media literacy as pivotal ATL skills for your product goal, reinforcing their relevance to project management and design.
The usability evaluation uses diverse interview feedback and links observations to your success criteria, effectively supporting your 6/8 score.
Your description of organisational strategies linked to the action plan shows how systematic planning supported product completion effectively.
Including a visual calendar (Figure 7) showcases your organisational approach and provides concrete evidence of how you managed timelines and tasks.
Your media literacy reflections describe analysing cookbooks broadly; strengthen this by specifying criteria you used (e.g., layout, tone, illustration style).
You reflect thoughtfully on skill development, noting how the project improved creative thinking, communication, organisation, and media literacyâshowing clear personal growth.
While you note overcoming procrastination with a new schedule, explicitly link how this revised strategy improved your workflow or recovery timeline.
You clearly outline knowledge expansion in book design, illustration techniques, geography, and cultural research, demonstrating breadth of learning impact.
Presenting a screenshot of the table of contents alongside a sample recipe page substantiates your claim of including eight cultural cuisines in the cookbook.
Your evaluation of recipe usefulness notes mixed feedback but stops short of actionable improvementsâidentify one or two specific recipe enhancements to address user concerns.
